data.title: System Design — Revision Log
data.description: Every subtopic from your SDE2 prep guide, tracked. Press Revised on a topic each time you go over it — the dot ages from green to red so you always know what's gone cold. Click into any subtopic for a deeper note and a reference link.

subs.d: One request per TCP connection; the connection closes after every response, so each request re-pays a full TCP (and TLS, if HTTPS) handshake. Fine for simple document fetches, brutal for pages with many assets.

Introduced Keep-Alive (reuse the TCP connection across requests) and pipelining (send several requests without waiting for each response). Pipelining still suffers application-layer head-of-line blocking: responses must return in the order requested, so a slow one blocks everything queued behind it — browsers worked around this by opening ~6 parallel connections per domain.

Binary framing instead of text, with multiplexing — many streams share one TCP connection, plus header compression (HPACK) and server push. Solves the application-layer HOL blocking of 1.1, but a single lost TCP packet still stalls every multiplexed stream, because TCP guarantees strictly in-order delivery.

Runs over QUIC (built on UDP) instead of TCP. QUIC does its own reliability and congestion control per-stream, so one stream's packet loss no longer blocks the others — fixing transport-layer HOL blocking. It also folds the TLS handshake into the connection handshake, cutting round trips (0-RTT/1-RTT resumption), which matters most on lossy mobile networks.

Starts life as a normal HTTP request with an `Upgrade: websocket` header; the server replies `101 Switching Protocols` and the connection becomes a persistent, full-duplex socket. You own state management from there — heartbeats (ping/pong), reconnect logic, and session affinity if you're load-balancing across many servers.
The client re-issues a fresh HTTP request on a fixed interval. Simple to build, but wasteful (most responses are empty) and its latency is bounded by however long the poll interval is.

The server holds the request open until new data exists (or a timeout hits), then responds immediately; the client re-issues right away. Better latency and efficiency than short polling with zero special infrastructure, but every cycle still pays full HTTP overhead and it complicates load-balancer/proxy timeout configuration.

A one-directional server→client stream over plain HTTP (`text/event-stream`); the browser's `EventSource` API auto-reconnects and resumes via `Last-Event-ID`. Much simpler than WebSockets when you only need push (live scores, notifications) since it rides on ordinary HTTP infrastructure — but there's no client→server channel on the same connection.

Each request carries all the context it needs — no server-side session reliance — which is what lets REST APIs scale horizontally behind any load balancer. URLs name resources (nouns, e.g. `/users/123/orders`); HTTP verbs express the action (GET/POST/PUT/PATCH/DELETE).

2xx success, 3xx redirection, 4xx client error (400 bad request, 401 unauthenticated, 403 unauthorized, 404 not found, 409 conflict, 429 rate limited), 5xx server error. Interviewers often probe whether you pick the *right* 4xx code, not just "it failed."

GET/PUT/DELETE are expected to be idempotent (repeating them has the same effect as doing it once); POST typically is not. For retry-safe writes (e.g. payments) use an explicit idempotency key so a network-retry doesn't double-charge.
A `.proto` schema compiles into strongly-typed client/server stubs across languages, giving smaller payloads and faster (de)serialization than JSON.
gRPC rides on HTTP/2, so it inherits multiplexing for free, and supports four call shapes: unary, server-streaming, client-streaming, and bidirectional streaming.
Better performance and strict typing make it a strong fit for internal service-to-service calls, but it's less human-readable/debuggable and needs a gRPC-Web proxy to reach browsers directly.
Instead of the consumer polling for updates, the provider calls a pre-registered callback URL the moment an event happens — decoupling the two systems and removing polling overhead entirely.

A production webhook receiver has to handle delivery failures (retry with backoff), duplicate deliveries (dedup via an idempotency key), out-of-order arrival (sequence numbers/versioning), and verify the sender's HMAC signature before trusting the payload.

A single endpoint where the client specifies exactly the fields it needs in a query — eliminating REST's over-fetching (unused extra fields) and under-fetching (needing several round trips to assemble one view).

The schema declares types/relationships; resolvers are functions that fetch each field's data, sometimes fanning out to several services — watch for the classic N+1 query problem, usually fixed by batching (DataLoader).
Mutations handle writes; subscriptions push real-time updates to clients, often carried over WebSockets under the hood.
Flexible ad hoc queries make simple URL-based HTTP caching (as REST enjoys) much harder, and deeply nested queries need query-cost analysis so a single request can't be used to abuse the server.

`LIMIT 20 OFFSET 100` — simple and supports jumping to an arbitrary page, but the database must scan/skip rows so it degrades at large offsets, and results shift or duplicate if rows are inserted/deleted between page loads.

Paginate from a stable pointer, e.g. `WHERE id > last_id ORDER BY id LIMIT 20`. Scales far better for high-volume/infinite-scroll feeds and stays stable under concurrent writes, at the cost of not being able to jump straight to page 47.
Dynamic search/filter UIs need composite indexes that match your common filter+sort combinations; range filters (dates, price bands) benefit from ordered B-tree indexes.
Sorting on a non-indexed or computed/aggregate column forces an expensive in-memory sort at query time — a common interview follow-up is "what if they want to sort by a derived field?"
Your own isolated virtual network inside AWS, with a CIDR range you control — the foundation every other networking construct sits inside.
Physically separate data centers within a region, linked by low-latency connections — deploying across multiple AZs is the standard way to survive a single data center failure.

Public subnets route out through an Internet Gateway (load balancers, bastion hosts live here); private subnets route outbound only via a NAT Gateway (app servers, databases), so databases are never directly reachable from the internet.

topics.title: Compute Virtualization: EC2, ASG, Security Groups

subs.d: EC2 gives you virtual machines sized for compute/memory/storage-optimized workloads; an ASG scales instance count up/down based on metrics like CPU or request count using a launch template.

Security Groups are stateful, instance-level firewalls (you only need allow rules — return traffic is automatically permitted). NACLs are stateless, subnet-level firewalls that need explicit allow *and* deny rules — a classic interview distinction.
Linux namespaces (PID, network, mount, etc.) isolate what a process can see; cgroups cap the resources it can use. Containers share the host kernel, which is what makes them lighter than full VMs.

Each Dockerfile instruction creates a cached layer, so ordering matters: put rarely-changing steps (dependency installs) before frequently-changing ones (copying app code) to maximize cache hits. Registries (Docker Hub, ECR) store and version the resulting images; pulling a large image adds to cold-start latency.

A Pod is the smallest deployable unit — one or more tightly-coupled containers sharing network/storage. The scheduler places pods based on resource requests/limits and affinity/anti-affinity/taint rules.

Deployments manage ReplicaSets to enable rolling updates and rollbacks. Services give a stable virtual IP/DNS name in front of a changing set of pods (pods have ephemeral IPs, which Services abstract away).
Ingress controllers do layer-7 host/path-based routing into the cluster (usually backed by an ALB or NGINX); ConfigMaps/Secrets externalize configuration from the container image itself.
A task definition is AWS's equivalent of a pod spec — CPU/memory, containers, networking mode. ECS Services keep a desired task count running and integrate with ALB target groups.

Serverless compute for containers — no EC2 instances to patch or manage, billed per task's vCPU/memory — trading away host-level control and often paying a higher per-unit cost than self-managed EC2 at large scale.
Karpenter watches for unschedulable pods and provisions right-sized nodes directly, which is faster and more flexible than the traditional cluster-autoscaler-plus-fixed-node-groups approach.

It continuously looks for chances to bin-pack pods onto fewer/cheaper nodes and kill underutilized ones, and can bid across many instance types on spot (70–90% cheaper, reclaimable with ~2 min notice) for fault-tolerant workloads while keeping on-demand for anything critical.
Graviton nodes often deliver 20–40% better price/performance for compatible workloads, but you need multi-arch images (`docker buildx`) and to verify all your dependencies actually have ARM builds.
Foreign keys enforce referential integrity across tables; indexes (typically B-tree) speed up lookups but cost extra storage and slow down writes.
Atomicity (all-or-nothing), Consistency (valid state transitions per constraints), Isolation (concurrent transactions don't interfere), Durability (committed data survives a crash).
Reading another transaction's *uncommitted* data — prevented at Read Committed and above.
Re-reading the same row twice in one transaction gives different values because another transaction committed a change in between — prevented at Repeatable Read and above.

Re-running the same range query returns a different *set* of rows because rows were inserted/deleted — prevented at Serializable (and often handled at Repeatable Read via next-key locking in engines like InnoDB).
Read Uncommitted → Read Committed → Repeatable Read → Serializable, each level trading throughput for fewer permitted anomalies. Memorize which anomaly each level actually blocks.
Writes go to the primary; reads can be spread across replicas — this scales read throughput but does nothing for write throughput.

Synchronous replication waits for a replica ack before confirming the write (strong consistency, higher latency, can block if a replica is slow). Asynchronous confirms immediately and lets replicas catch up later (lower latency, but replicas can serve stale reads and an unreplicated write can be lost on primary failure).
Partitioning data across multiple DB instances by a sharding key (e.g. user_id) scales both reads and writes horizontally.

Nodes and keys are hashed onto the same ring; a key belongs to the next node clockwise. Adding/removing a node only remaps the keys in its immediate neighborhood, not the whole keyspace — a huge win over naive `hash % N`, which reshuffles almost everything whenever N changes.
Each physical node claims many points on the ring, smoothing out load distribution and letting heterogeneous-capacity nodes get proportional shares.

Partitioning by ranges of a key (e.g. one shard per month) is great for time-series/append-heavy workloads and easy range scans, but can create a "hot" shard when all current writes land on "today" — usually mitigated by combining it with other sharding techniques.
Schema-less documents suit evolving/heterogeneous data shapes; multikey indexes let you index nested fields and arrays directly.
An ordered log of write operations that secondaries tail to stay in sync — conceptually similar to a write-ahead log.
All nodes are peers (no single primary) — placement uses consistent hashing, and a gossip protocol handles cluster membership.

The partition key decides which node(s) own a row; clustering columns decide the sort order *within* that partition. Cassandra has no ad hoc joins, so you model tables around your queries, not your entities.

Deletes are markers, not immediate removals (replicas need time to converge), cleaned up later during compaction. Too many tombstones degrade read performance — nicknamed "tombstone hell," a real production gotcha.

Data is modeled as nodes and relationships, both carrying properties. Traversal cost is roughly proportional to the subgraph actually visited, not the whole dataset — which is why relational joins get prohibitively expensive for the same query (friend-of-friend, fraud rings, recommendation paths) that graph traversal handles cheaply.

Data is organized/sharded by time range with heavy write-optimized ingestion and high compression (delta encoding, since consecutive metric values are usually similar) — built for "give me values for X between time A and B," not random point lookups.
Objects live in a flat key-value space (no true directories, just key prefixes simulating folders); versioning keeps multiple copies of an object for recovery/audit.
Bucket policies + IAM + ACLs control access; cross-region replication improves durability and gives lower-latency reads closer to users.
Client-side (browser/app) caches are the fastest but hardest to invalidate centrally; CDNs cache static (sometimes dynamic) content at edge locations near users, cutting origin load and latency.

In-process caches (e.g. a local LRU map) are extremely fast but not shared across instances and vanish on restart. A distributed cache (Redis/Memcached) is shared fleet-wide and stays consistent, at the cost of one extra network hop.

The app checks the cache first; on a miss it reads the DB and populates the cache. Simple and only caches what's actually requested, but the first request after a miss/eviction is slow and there's a brief staleness window.
Writes go to the cache and the DB synchronously — the cache is always fresh, at the cost of extra write latency.
Writes hit the cache immediately and the DB updates asynchronously — fast writes, but a cache failure before the flush means data loss.
Evicts the least-recently-accessed item — a good general-purpose default, implemented in O(1) with a hash map plus a doubly linked list.
Evicts the least-frequently-accessed item — better when access *frequency* predicts future use more than recency, but needs an aging mechanism so stale "popular" items don't stick around forever.
Evicts in pure insertion order regardless of usage — simplest to implement, but ignores actual access patterns so it's rarely the optimal choice.
Round Robin cycles through servers in order — fine when servers/requests are roughly uniform. Weighted Round Robin assigns weights so a bigger instance gets proportionally more traffic.
Routes to whichever server currently has the fewest active connections — better than Round Robin when request durations vary a lot.
The same client is consistently routed to the same server — gives you session affinity without a shared session store, but can create imbalance if client IPs are skewed.

ALBs scale their own capacity gradually, so a sudden massive spike (a flash sale, a viral post) can outrun that scale-up — AWS explicitly recommends contacting support ahead of predictable huge spikes to pre-warm.
Long-lived connections (WebSockets) need very different idle-timeout tuning than short-lived REST calls — a real config gotcha, not just a theoretical one.

Nodes and keys are hashed onto the same circular space; a key belongs to the first node found walking clockwise from its position. Node churn only remaps the keys between it and its predecessor — far less data movement than modulo hashing.

Giving each physical node many virtual positions turns one node's share of keys into the sum of many small scattered arcs instead of one big contiguous one — improving balance and letting bigger nodes claim proportionally more vnodes.
Point-to-point delivers a message to exactly one consumer (task distribution); pub-sub broadcasts a copy to every subscriber (e.g. order-placed fanning out to inventory, notifications, analytics).
Multiple consumer instances split the work of a topic/queue, with each message processed by exactly one group member — this is how you scale consumption horizontally.
Pull (consumer polls, e.g. Kafka) gives the consumer control over pacing and backpressure; push (broker sends) can overwhelm a slow consumer without careful flow control.

The commit log is an append-only, immutable log per partition — Kafka's core abstraction. A topic's partitions parallelize throughput, but ordering is only guaranteed *within* a partition, never across the whole topic.
Each consumer group tracks its own read position per partition (stored in an internal Kafka topic), which enables independent consumption rates and full replay.
Controls durability — e.g. replication factor 3 tolerates 2 broker failures without data loss, assuming an appropriate `min.insync.replicas` setting.

Ingests high-speed binary market-data feeds from an exchange, normalizes formats across sources, then broadcasts to subscribers via Redis Pub/Sub for low-latency fan-out. Redis Pub/Sub is fire-and-forget (non-durable), so it's right for live updates but wrong if you need guaranteed delivery or replay.
You can't atomically write to a database *and* publish to a message broker in one transaction — they're two separate systems.

Write the event into an outbox table inside the *same* DB transaction as the business change, then a separate process (a log-tailing connector like Debezium, or a polling publisher) reads that table and publishes to the broker — giving at-least-once delivery consistent with the DB state without a distributed transaction.

Tracks a dependency's failure rate over a sliding window; once failures exceed a threshold the breaker "opens" and fails fast with a fallback instead of hammering the failing dependency. A half-open state periodically lets a trial request through to test recovery before fully closing again.
The foundational (but notoriously hard to implement correctly) algorithm for getting nodes to agree on a single value despite failures, using proposal numbers and majority quorums.

Designed to be more understandable than Paxos — explicitly separates leader election (majority vote wins a term), log replication (leader appends and replicates, commits once a majority ack), and the safety rule that an entry is only committed/visible once durably replicated to a quorum.
With N nodes, a majority quorum (⌊N/2⌋+1) guarantees any two quorums overlap by at least one node — this is exactly what prevents split-brain and keeps consistency across leader changes.

Provides a hierarchical, filesystem-like namespace with strongly consistent reads/writes (via its own Zab protocol) — used for leader election through ephemeral sequential znodes, configuration management, and distributed locks.
Solves similar problems (service discovery, health checks, KV store, leader election) built on Raft, with first-class service-mesh integration.
A coordinator asks all participants to "prepare" (lock resources, confirm they can commit); if everyone says yes, it tells them all to commit in phase two — otherwise, abort.

2PC guarantees atomicity across services/databases but is blocking: if the coordinator crashes right after phase one, participants can be stuck holding locks indefinitely — a major availability weakness, especially under network partitions.

A long-running distributed transaction (e.g. checkout spanning payment, inventory, shipping) is broken into a sequence of local transactions, each with a compensating transaction to undo it if a later step fails.

Orchestration uses a central coordinator that explicitly tells each service what to do and calls compensations on failure (easier to reason about/debug). Choreography has each service react to others' events with no central coordinator (more decoupled, but harder to trace end to end).
Sagas give up strong atomicity for eventual consistency and availability — a very common interview discussion: "payment succeeded but inventory reservation failed — now what?"

A JWT has a header (algorithm), payload (claims — user id, roles, expiry), and signature (integrity check, signed with a secret or private key). Because it's self-contained, any server holding the verification key can validate it without a shared session store — great for horizontal scaling.
Access tokens should be short-lived (minutes) to limit damage from a leak; a longer-lived refresh token (often an httpOnly cookie) mints new access tokens without forcing re-login.
You can't invalidate a JWT before its natural expiry without extra infrastructure like a blocklist — a classic trade-off vs. traditional server-side sessions.

The standard, most secure flow for server-side apps: the client redirects the user to the auth server, gets a short-lived code, and exchanges it server-side (with a client secret) for tokens — the token never transits the browser directly.

Implicit returned the token directly in the redirect URL fragment for pure client-side apps — insecure, now superseded by Authorization Code + PKCE. Client credentials is for machine-to-machine auth, no user involved.
OAuth's core job is authorization ("what can you do"); OpenID Connect layers authentication ("who are you") on top — a frequent clarifying question in interviews.
A central Identity Provider authenticates the user once; multiple applications trust that assertion (via SAML or OIDC) instead of each maintaining its own credential store.
One place to disable an account revokes access everywhere — simpler user management, but the IdP becomes a single point of failure and a high-value attack target for the whole ecosystem.

Modern bot defense analyzes behavioral signals (mouse movement, typing patterns, browsing history) to produce a risk score, only challenging suspicious traffic — frictionless for legitimate users instead of a puzzle for everyone.
Groups similar traffic patterns to spot coordinated bot/fraud campaigns, rather than judging each request in isolation.
Sits in front of the app, filtering on rules like SQLi/XSS signatures, rate limits, geo-blocking, and IP reputation.
Paired with a CDN, absorbs/filters volumetric attacks before they ever reach origin servers.

Logstash (or lighter agents like Filebeat) collects, parses, and transforms logs — e.g. grok patterns pulling structured fields out of raw text — before shipping them. Elasticsearch indexes logs for fast full-text search via inverted indexes, and Kibana builds dashboards/alerts on top.
A functionally similar scalable search/analytics engine born from an Elasticsearch/Kibana license fork — commonly used to avoid Elastic's licensing terms, and available as an AWS-managed service.
Functions triggered directly by log/metric events (e.g. new CloudWatch Logs entries) can extract metrics, trigger alerts, or transform data before storage — no standing server needed.
A trace ID follows one logical request across every microservice it touches; each span is one unit of work (a service call, a DB query) inside it, with parent-child relationships forming a tree.

Traces show *where* latency is spent across the call graph, logs give error context, metrics tell you *when* and how broadly something is impacted — practice explaining how you'd use all three to debug a P99 latency spike.

Encodes a lat/long pair into a base-32 string where shared prefixes mean spatial proximity, enabling range queries on a single-dimension index — with known edge-case discontinuities at cell boundaries.
Projects the sphere onto cube faces and uses a Hilbert curve for locality-preserving 64-bit cell IDs — better proximity behavior than geohash, without the boundary discontinuity problem.
A hexagonal grid where every cell has 6 equidistant neighbors (unlike squares), simplifying neighborhood traversal and radius queries, with a hierarchical cell structure for multi-resolution lookups.
Sharding a search index (e.g. Elasticsearch) by geo-cell means a proximity query only has to hit the relevant shards instead of scanning everything.

Returning exact distances/coordinates lets an attacker triangulate a user's real location with a handful of fake queries — mitigated by fuzzing displayed coordinates and computing precise distance only server-side.

User-based finds users with similar rating patterns and recommends what they liked; item-based finds items frequently co-engaged with ("customers who bought X also bought Y") and is generally more stable/scalable since item-item similarity drifts more slowly than user-user similarity.
Measures the angle between two rating vectors, ignoring magnitude — so a user who rates everything a 5 and one who rates everything a 3 can still register as "similar" in pattern.

Decomposes the sparse user-item interaction matrix into two lower-rank user-factor and item-factor matrices whose product approximates the original ratings, capturing latent factors (like genre affinity) that aren't explicitly labeled.
Classical SVD is exact but expensive; modern systems typically use gradient-descent-based factorization like Alternating Least Squares (ALS), which scales far better to large sparse datasets.

Storing dense user/item embeddings in Redis (or a dedicated vector DB) gets millisecond-latency retrieval at recommendation-serving time — trading memory cost for speed versus recomputing or fetching from a cold store.
Builds a multi-layer navigable-small-world graph enabling fast greedy search from coarse to fine layers — a very popular recall/speed trade-off used in many vector databases.
Google's approach combines vector quantization with an anisotropic loss function tuned specifically to preserve similarity-ranking accuracy rather than just minimizing reconstruction error.

Sliding-window counts (e.g. tweets/sec over the last 60s) are computed incrementally as new events arrive and old ones age out — avoiding a full recompute each tick, a core building block for trend/anomaly detection.
Processes data in small time-bounded batches — a practical middle ground between true per-event streaming (like Flink) and the simplicity of batch processing.
Maps each term to the list of documents containing it — the reverse of a normal document-to-terms mapping, and the foundation of full-text search engines.

A query is broadcast to every relevant shard holding part of the index, each returns its local top results, and a coordinator merges/ranks them into the final result — this pattern recurs across distributed search systems.
Large video files are split into parts, uploaded (possibly in parallel), with per-chunk hash checks for integrity and support for resuming an interrupted upload instead of restarting from zero.

An upload triggers a directed-acyclic-graph of jobs — audio extraction, thumbnail generation, multi-resolution transcoding — where independent steps run concurrently and dependent steps wait appropriately.

Video is pre-encoded into multiple quality tiers, split into short segments, and listed in a manifest (HLS `.m3u8` or MPEG-DASH `.mpd`); the player dynamically switches quality based on measured network conditions, with segments cached at CDN edges near viewers.
User profile data is partitioned across PostGIS-enabled PostgreSQL (strong consistency for core profile data) alongside Cassandra (high-write-volume data like swipe events).
Swipes are logged asynchronously so they never block the swipe UX; when a mutual like occurs, a match event streams through Kafka to trigger notification delivery.

Since each user holds a long-lived socket, the hard problem is routing a message to whichever server holds the *recipient's* socket — solved with a registry of "which server holds which user's connection" plus Redis Pub/Sub to route across the fleet.
Messages for offline users are queued in a NoSQL store until reconnection, with a push notification via APNs (Apple) / FCM (Android) to alert the user even when the app is closed.
Tweets are ingested into Kafka, then a Spark Streaming pipeline runs sentiment analysis and computes rolling-window aggregations (e.g. hashtag frequency per minute) to surface trending topics.
Twitter's real-time search index used scatter-gather across sharded inverted indexes to serve low-latency full-text search over a constantly-updating firehose.
Consumes raw binary exchange feeds, normalizes, and broadcasts live prices via Redis — usually the single most latency-critical component in the whole system.

Order execution and balance updates need strong consistency; the transactional outbox pattern keeps "order executed" DB writes and their downstream event stream consistent without a fragile distributed transaction.
EKS clusters autoscale with Karpenter, mixing spot and Graviton nodes for less-critical background work while keeping the critical trading path on stable on-demand instances.
Product metadata/categories are indexed in Elasticsearch so users can filter by multiple facets (brand, price, rating) simultaneously with fast aggregated counts per facet value.
A write-through Redis cache sits in front of the catalog DB so high-read, low-write listing pages never hit the database on every request.
Pre-generates unique base-62 short keys in batches (stored in a pool) so the write path never has to check for collisions in real time — a classic URL-shortener building block.
Metadata (title, owner, timestamps, key) lives in a relational DB for fast lookups/indexing, while the actual (potentially large) text payload is stored in S3.
Front queues prioritize URLs by freshness/importance; back queues enforce politeness — typically one queue per host with a minimum delay between requests to any single site.

Resolve DNS (often cached locally), download the HTML, extract links to feed back into the frontier, and use a Bloom filter to cheaply check "have we seen this URL?" without storing every URL in memory.

Separate SQS queues (or SNS topics fanning out to SQS) for promotional vs. transactional notifications, so a flood of marketing messages can never delay time-critical ones like OTPs or security alerts.

Failed deliveries retry with exponential backoff plus jitter (jitter avoids synchronized retry storms); persistently failing messages route to a Dead Letter Queue; circuit breakers isolate a failing downstream provider so it can't degrade the whole pipeline.

A fast hot path runs a lightweight GBDT model for millisecond real-time risk scoring on every transaction, while a slower cold path runs deeper Neo4j graph analysis to catch collusion/fraud rings the hot path misses.

Sliding-window "velocity" features (e.g. transactions from this account in the last 5 minutes) and device fingerprint matching (the same physical device behind many accounts) are strong, widely-used fraud signals.
